label: ai
topic: Satirical argument that university student government should be replaced with a monarchy
text:
Every spring, our campus is afflicted by a seasonal plague far more devastating than finals week: student government elections. You literally cannot walk to the dining hall without being accosted by a desperately smiling sophomore in an ill-fitting blazer, thrusting a glossy flyer into your hands. They promise to "revolutionize the meal plan," "foster transparent dialogue," and "empower the student voice." It is all, of course, a spectacular lie. After weeks of aggressive campaigning and spam emails, the newly elected student senate will spend the entire academic year locked in tedious parliamentary procedure, fiercely debating whether the campus squirrel-watching club deserves an extra fifty dollars in their budget. Our student government is a bloated theater of the absurd, a resume-padding bureaucracy for aspiring law students that achieves absolutely nothing of substance. I propose a much simpler, far more elegant solution. It is time to abolish the democratic experiment on this campus and replace the student government with an absolute monarchy.

Thomas Hobbes had it right all along. In his 1651 masterpiece Leviathan, Hobbes argued that without a strong, undivided sovereign, human beings are doomed to a chaotic state of nature where life is "solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and short." Look around our campus. Without a sovereign, we are trapped in our own collegiate state of nature, viciously fighting over study room reservations in the library and enduring the agonizing tyranny of endless committee meetings about sustainability. We do not need fifty squabbling senators drafting meaningless resolutions that the university administration will simply ignore; we need one glorious, unquestioned ruler to impose order upon the chaotic masses and actually get things done. Think of the sheer efficiency of a royal decree. A monarch doesn't need to hold a town hall forum to decide if the campus Wi-Fi needs fixing; they simply command it, under penalty of treason for the IT department.

The selection process for our new campus sovereign would be far more entertaining than our current electoral circus. Instead of voting via a neglected web portal that inevitably crashes, we could determine the ruler through a trial by combat in the main quad. Alternatively, we could see who is worthy enough to pull a ceremonial sword from a perfectly preserved block of dining hall meatloaf. No more campaign slogans, no more QR codes chalked onto the sidewalks—just the raw, undeniable mandate of heaven, or at least the mandate of surviving the quad combat.

Once crowned, our campus monarch would naturally be entitled to certain privileges befitting their divine right to rule. To start, the sovereign should possess the absolute power of eminent domain over any dorm room. If the king or queen fancies your corner suite with the nice view of the city, you must immediately pack your things and relocate to a converted broom closet in the freshman dorms. Furthermore, the monarch must be transported between their lectures in a majestic golf cart chariot, which will be physically pulled by the defeated candidates of the erstwhile student government.

Most importantly, the monarch would possess the ultimate power of banishment. This power would be wielded primarily against students who walk too slowly in groups of four across the narrowest sidewalks on campus, blocking everyone else who is late to class. Under the new regime, these slow-walking offenders would be instantly exiled to the satellite campus, never to return. Some overly sensitive political science majors might call this tyranny, but I call it streamlined governance. Democracy on a college campus has proven to be a failed, exhausting experiment, yielding nothing but inbox spam and unfulfilled promises of better toilet paper in the residential halls. A monarchy offers pageantry, decisive action, and a unified aesthetic. We would no longer be mere students; we would be loyal subjects. So, the next time a polished twenty-year-old asks for your vote to become the vice president of internal affairs, politely decline. Tell them you are saving your loyalty for the crown, and that you look forward to bowing to them when they are pulling the royal chariot.
